video_title: "Crash Pricing" Setting In As Distressed Home Sellers Capitulate | Nick Gerli, Reventure
gist: Real estate analyst Nick Gerli tells Thoughtful Money's Adam Taggart that the four-year U.S. housing demand collapse persists at 2008-09 levels, but distressed sellers in Sun Belt states like Florida, Texas and Georgia have begun accepting 'crash pricing' cuts of $100,000-$150,000, while Rust Belt markets (Illinois, Ohio, Michigan) see prices still rising as out-migration eases. Gerli walks through his own case study of buying an Atlanta townhouse 34% below its 2023 sale price and argues national prices still need to fall another 15-20% to normalize, with a stock-market correction or unemployment spike likely to accelerate that reset.

themes.theme: Sun Belt Distress Pricing Emerges After Years of Sticky Prices

insights.claim: The current U.S. housing correction began roughly four years earlier (around 2022) when home sales and buyer demand collapsed to record lows, and as of mid-2026 key demand indicators, existing home sales, pending home sales, builder supply, and mortgage applications, remain at 2008-2009 recession levels.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Confirms the demand-side downturn is not over; investors should not assume a demand recovery is imminent despite four years of decline.
insights.quote: we're literally still at 2008 2009 lows in terms of demand.

insights.claim: National average home prices stayed elevated for years despite record-low transaction volume because sellers simply refused to cut list prices while waiting out the market, and the few buyers who did transact skewed toward wealthier, higher-end purchasers, which propped up average sale prices even as the broader market froze.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Explains why headline home-price indices understated the depth of the underlying demand collapse; investors relying on median/average price data alone were misled.

insights.claim: In the past six months, distressed sellers have begun accepting 'crash pricing,' with Nick Gerli finding individual listings down $100,000-$150,000 from their 2022 sale prices in Sun Belt states, a shift from the prior multi-year pattern of stubborn sellers refusing to cut prices.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Signals the start of a distressed-selling phase that could accelerate further price discovery in these markets.
insights.quote: Literally crash pricing is now starting to set in on certain listings.

insights.claim: States including Florida, Texas, Tennessee, Georgia, Arizona, and Colorado now have the most housing supply on the market since 2012 (the tail end of the last housing downturn), with near-daily new listings showing sellers taking large losses.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Rising inventory in these states is the leading indicator for where buyer negotiating leverage and discounts will keep expanding.

insights.claim: A three-bed, two-bath, 1,900-sq-ft new-build house in Forney, Texas (near Dallas) that had a pending sale price of about $388,000 in November 2022 was listed in 2026 for $216,000 ($116/sq ft), a roughly 45% decline and a 30-40% discount to replacement cost.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: A concrete data point showing new-build Sun Belt housing already trading near 2017-2018 price levels, useful as a comp for underwriting similar distressed listings.

insights.claim: A flipped three-bed, three-bath house in Atlanta that sold for about $330,000 in 2023 was listed in 2026 for roughly $189,000-$190,000, a decline of over 40%, marking the first time in years that homes have listed below $200,000 in that market.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Shows distress pricing is not limited to new builds bought at the 2022 peak, but is also hitting properties bought as recently as 2023.

themes.theme: Record Mortgage Debt Burdens Are Driving Forced Selling

insights.claim: The average back-end debt-to-income ratio on new U.S. mortgage originations reached 39.6% in 2025 per Fannie Mae's national mortgage database, exceeding the 38.7% peak recorded during the 2007 housing bubble, meaning today's average mortgage borrower carries a heavier payment burden relative to income than borrowers did just before the Global Financial Crisis.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Undercuts the narrative that post-2008 underwriting reforms made current mortgages safe; investors should treat current mortgage credit quality as comparable to or worse than the 2007 bubble on a payment-burden basis.
insights.quote: In 2025, 39.6% was the average backend debt to income ratio for all mortgage originations.

insights.claim: Government-backed lenders (Fannie Mae, FHA) have progressively loosened debt-to-income underwriting limits over the past 20-25 years, from a threshold where spending more than about 35% of income on mortgage debt was once unthinkable for a government-backed loan to now permitting up to roughly 50%, even though only the borrower's income, not payment affordability, is what gets verified.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific regulatory/underwriting mechanism, not just rates, that is embedding fragility into the current mortgage market and could amplify forced selling in a downturn.

insights.claim: Total housing costs for existing U.S. homeowners, mortgage payment, property taxes, insurance, and maintenance combined, rose almost 40% between 2019 and 2025 according to a Wall Street Journal analysis, meaning even owners with a locked-in sub-3% mortgage rate are facing meaningfully higher carrying costs than when they bought.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Explains why forced-selling pressure can rise even without mortgage rate resets, since insurance and tax inflation alone are squeezing owner cash flow.
insights.quote: the payments of existing owners are up almost 40% the last six years.

insights.claim: The mortgage rate 'lock-in effect' is structurally decaying: the share of existing U.S. mortgages carrying a rate of 6% or higher has more than tripled over the past four years and now exceeds the share of mortgages below 3%, so a shrinking proportion of owners are actually insulated from today's higher rates.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: As the low-rate cohort keeps shrinking, more owners each year lose their reason to stay put, which should gradually increase listing supply and forced-sale pressure, a trend to track rather than a one-time shock.

insights.claim: In Q1 2026 the market rate for a new mortgage was 6.2% versus a 4.4% average effective rate for existing owners (a 1.8-point gap), down from a peak gap of 3.1 points in Q3 2023; before the pandemic (2013-2021) this relationship was inverted, existing owners could often lower their payment by moving and taking a new mortgage, a structural tailwind for buyer mobility that has now reversed into a headwind.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Quantifies why housing 'churn' (trade-up/relocation demand) is unlikely to recover soon: moving still means taking on a materially higher rate than staying put, even as the gap slowly narrows.

themes.theme: Demand Stays Frozen, But Sellers Are the Real Story to Watch

insights.claim: Nick Gerli expects depressed U.S. home-buyer demand to persist for roughly another four years, matching the four years already elapsed since the correction began, because existing low-rate owners have no financial incentive to move, renters face roughly a 40% higher monthly payment to buy versus rent at current prices, and demographic tailwinds are fading (falling births, rising deaths).
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Sets a multi-year time horizon for investors betting on housing-linked assets (homebuilders, mortgage originators, real estate services) not to expect a demand-driven recovery soon.
insights.quote: I think we could just continue to see this low demand that we've now seen for four years. I think we could see it for another four years.

insights.claim: Gerli argues the housing market isn't best described as 'frozen' but as 'disinterested' with inertia, buyers see no financial upside to purchasing given current valuations, mortgage rates, and demographics, which he frames as a distinct and more durable condition than a temporary freeze.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Reframes the demand problem as structural rather than cyclical, implying it won't resolve simply once rates ease modestly.
insights.quote: I would just say disinterested with a massive amount of inertia explains the current American sentiment towards buying a house.

insights.claim: Gerli frames the unfolding correction using an 'avalanche' analogy: a small number of distressed sellers listing and cutting prices can prompt other financially stretched owners to list preemptively for fear of being 'too late,' creating a self-reinforcing cycle, though he says whether it actually turns into a full avalanche depends on the broader economy, e.g., a spike in unemployment or a stock market correction.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Gives investors a trigger-based framework, unemployment spiking toward 6-6.5% or a stock market correction, for when to expect the correction to accelerate rather than continue grinding slowly.

insights.claim: Gerli describes most existing sellers as 'delusional' about comparable sales, routinely dismissing nearby distressed sale prices as irrelevant to their own home, and argues that only macro shock headlines (recession, unemployment spikes, a 30% stock market decline) are likely to break that psychology and force broader price capitulation.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Suggests price discovery will stay slow and localized absent a macro catalyst, useful for timing entry into distressed housing plays.
insights.quote: if there's headlines in the Wall Street Journal and CNBC about unemployment rate spikes the US is in a recession stock market down 30%. I think that wakes those sellers up.

insights.claim: A seller's willingness to negotiate correlates strongly with their existing mortgage rate: sellers holding roughly 7% rates are far more open to lowball offers than sellers with roughly 3% rates, because the low-rate seller's imputed cost of giving up that mortgage often outweighs the discount a buyer is requesting, making them more likely to reject offers outright.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Gives buyers a targeting heuristic, use public/mortgage-lookup data to filter for high-rate sellers before spending time negotiating.

themes.theme: The Most Bifurcated Housing Market in History

insights.claim: Gerli describes the current U.S. housing market as the most bifurcated it has ever been: Sun Belt/Mountain West states are seeing falling prices and rising inventory while Northeast/Midwest states with persistent housing shortages (e.g., New York, New Jersey, Connecticut, Illinois) continue to see prices rise, making broad 'national housing market' commentary of limited use to buyers.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Reinforces that housing-related trades or purchase decisions need to be made at the state/metro/zip-code level, not off national headline data.

insights.claim: Florida's home-price correction is driven less by absolute migration levels than by the rate of change in migration: net domestic in-migration to Florida fell from 311,000 in 2022 to just 23,000 in 2025, the fourth-lowest level in 35 years, even though Florida still has net positive migration overall.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Shows that a still-positive net migration figure can mask a sharp deceleration that is enough to flip a market from appreciating to correcting, an important nuance for reading migration data.
insights.quote: in 2022, we had 311,000 net moves to Florida. Net moves in minus out. That was only 23,000 in 2025.

insights.claim: States long associated with population loss, Illinois, Ohio, Wisconsin, and Michigan, are seeing domestic out-migration shrink to multi-decade lows or turn positive (Ohio +11,900 net migration in 2025, its first strongly positive year in 35 years; Michigan positive in 2025 for the first time since 1991), and combined with historically low new-home construction in those states, this is now driving above-average price growth, Illinois has four of the top eight home-price-growth markets in the U.S.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific, underappreciated regional reversal: Rust Belt states with tight existing supply could keep outperforming Sun Belt markets on price even without a migration 'boom,' just a slowdown in outflows.

insights.claim: Austin, Texas home values are down 26% from their peak, and Gerli's home-price-to-income model now shows Austin as undervalued (back near 2016-2017 levels) even though he still expects a further decline toward a total peak-to-trough drop of 30-35%, making Austin one of the markets his model flags as approaching a buying opportunity.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete example of a formerly overheated pandemic-boom market cycling from overvalued to fundamentally undervalued, a pattern investors could look to replicate in other 2020-2021 boomtowns.

insights.claim: Downtown San Francisco (San Francisco County) home values corrected earlier than most markets right after the pandemic and are now among the lowest home-price-to-income ratios of the past 20 years, with values turning back upward over the last six months, which Gerli attributes to the AI boom, leading his model to flag the market as undervalued with a positive one-year forecast.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Flags San Francisco as a market where AI-driven job/income growth is already showing up in real estate demand data, a leading indicator investors can cross-check against other AI-boom-exposed markets.

insights.claim: Gerli considers Reventure's one-year state-level home-price forecast the single most useful metric for prospective buyers because it indicates conditions after a purchase closes; states forecast for outsized declines (e.g., Colorado -6%, Washington -6%) signal real negotiating leverage, while states already up strongly year-over-year and still labeled 'red' in his model (e.g., New York, Wisconsin, Illinois up about 5%, Connecticut up 4.8%) are unlikely to offer discounts and may be worth buying sooner rather than waiting.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Gives a specific, actionable screening rule (forecast direction over current YoY change) for deciding whether to wait for a discount or buy now before further appreciation.

themes.theme: A Field-Tested Playbook for Buying at a Deep Discount

insights.claim: Nick Gerli personally purchased an Atlanta townhouse (built 2021, previously sold for $497,000 in December 2023 and listed as low as $410,000 after 6-8 months on market with multiple price cuts) for $330,000, a 34% discount to the 2023 sale price and about 25% below its 2021 new-build price, by opening with a $315,000 offer and negotiating through two rounds of counters.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: A concrete, verifiable proof point that six-figure discounts are achievable right now in select Sun Belt markets for buyers willing to underwrite and negotiate aggressively.

insights.claim: Gerli's formula for finding the biggest discounts is to target listings where the seller bought at a peak price with a high mortgage rate, the property has already been on market 6+ months with multiple price cuts, and the zip code is one Reventure's data forecasts to keep declining, arguing this combination signals a seller who is 'willing to play ball' regardless of the current list price.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Gives buyers a concrete, replicable screening checklist for sourcing distressed-but-viable deals rather than relying on list price alone.

insights.claim: To win favorable negotiations, Gerli recommends establishing credibility and urgency upfront: send proof of funds (cash) or mortgage pre-approval plus documented down-payment liquidity to the listing agent before touring, offer a fast closing timeline as leverage, then anchor with a low offer and expect it to be rejected, treating any counteroffer, even a small one, as a signal the seller is negotiable.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: A tactical, reusable negotiation script for buyers trying to extract deep discounts from stressed sellers.
insights.quote: And if they do a counter, it's a win even if it's way off from what you want.

insights.claim: Gerli advises buyers to ignore the list price entirely on listings that look distressed (6+ months on market, prior price cuts) and instead underwrite the property independently, using cap-rate and rental-cash-flow analysis, to determine what offer makes sense, arguing many sellers are privately willing to transact well below their posted list price because they fear it won't sell if listed that low upfront.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Encourages buyers to submit low, fundamentals-based offers rather than self-selecting out of a deal because the sticker price looks too high.

insights.claim: homes.com (owned by CoStar) is, per Gerli, the only one of the four major U.S. listing websites that displays a property's existing mortgage details (rate, origination date, and remaining balance) directly on the listing, which he uses to identify sellers likely to be under payment stress before making an offer.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: A specific, actionable data source buyers can add to their research toolkit to pre-screen sellers for negotiating leverage.

themes.theme: Macro Catalysts That Could Accelerate the Reset

insights.claim: Gerli estimates that to clear the market at a 'normal' level of buyer demand, national home prices would need to fall roughly 15-20% further on average, implying that today's aggregate list/reported sale prices reflect a 'suspension of disbelief' among sellers rather than prices the market could actually absorb at scale.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Puts a specific magnitude on the remaining correction needed for the housing market to normalize, a benchmark for judging how much of the reset has already happened versus how much remains.
insights.quote: prices are going to have to be like 20% 15 to 20% lower on average.

insights.claim: Gerli argues a 20%+ stock market correction and/or unemployment rising to around 6-6.5% would be among the most effective catalysts for breaking sellers' price complacency, potentially triggering rapid price resets, especially for owners of second/third homes and higher-priced properties, by piercing the psychological sense of financial invincibility many sellers currently hold.
insights.speaker: Nick Gerli
insights.so_what: Identifies specific macro trigger levels (unemployment ~6-6.5%, a 20%+ equity drawdown) investors can monitor as leading indicators for an acceleration in housing price declines.

insights.claim: Host Adam Taggart frames a potential AI-bubble-driven stock market downturn as having 'option value' for patient home buyers, since it could bring housing prices down faster, while cautioning that such a downturn is not guaranteed to be a net benefit for buyers since it would also threaten their income and other assets during the same period.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Highlights the tension for prospective buyers between waiting for a bigger discount versus the personal financial risk of the very downturn that would produce it.
